historicalBlurb: Freddye Scarborough Henderson was the first African American to study Fashion Merchandising at her school and helped organize a major fashion show for African Americans in 1953. She later opened a travel service in Atlanta with her husband, specializing in cultural trips to Africa. She made a lasting historical impact by arranging Dr. MLK Jr.'s trip to receive the Nobel Peace Prize and was honored by the government of Ghana with the title "Queen Mother of Travel and Tours."

John Lewis was a key leader in the Civil Rights Movement and a longtime U.S. Representative for Georgia's 5th Congressional District, known for his unwavering commitment to justice and democratic participation.

Lillian Miles Lewis earned her English degree from California State College and a Master's from the University of Southern California. She worked as a librarian and later Associate Director at Atlanta University, where she met John Lewis in 1967, supporting his political career including his 1986 congressional race.

Horace Julian Bond was a civil rights leader, politician, and educator who co-founded the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC) while at Morehouse College. He organized protests against segregation and was elected to the Georgia House of Representatives in 1965, though he was initially denied his seat for opposing the Vietnam War until the U.S. Supreme Court intervened. He went on to serve decades in the Georgia General Assembly and later chaired the NAACP. Bond is buried at South-View Cemetery, a historic Atlanta burial ground founded in 1886 by formerly enslaved African Americans, where he rests alongside other notable leaders in the fight for equality.

Jean Childs Young was an educator and advocate dedicated to civil rights, public education, and children's welfare. She founded the Mayor's Task Force on Public Education and chaired the International Year of the Child under President Carter, earning her the title "heartbeat" of the movement. She is interred at South-View Cemetery, a historic burial ground founded in 1886 by formerly enslaved African Americans alongside many other civil rights leaders.

Graham W. Jackson Sr. was a legendary Atlanta musician whose poignant performance of 'Going Home' at President Franklin D. Roosevelt's funeral became an iconic symbol of American grief. A trailblazer in a segregated era, his artistic excellence earned him national acclaim and highlighted the profound cultural influence of Atlanta's Black community.
Known as 'Daddy King,' he was a powerful pastor and civil rights leader who fought for voting rights, equal pay, and racial justice at Ebenezer Baptist Church.
A pioneering civil rights leader and the first Black woman elected to the Georgia General Assembly, she advocated for education reform, social justice, and equal opportunity.
Known as the 'Mayor of Auburn Avenue,' Dobbs was a civic leader who championed voting rights and economic empowerment for African Americans in Atlanta.

Ludie Clay Andrews was a pioneering medical professional who broke racial barriers in Georgia's healthcare system, serving as the first African American registered nurse in the state. Her decades of leadership at Spelman College's MacVicar Hospital laid the groundwork for future generations of Black healthcare providers in the South.
In 1934, she began her work at the Auburn Branch of the Atlanta Public Library. She helped build a collection that preserved Black history, culture, and scholarship for future generations.

Herman J. Russell was a groundbreaking entrepreneur who grew his father's small plastering business into one of the largest Black-owned construction firms in the country. Despite facing racial barriers in securing loans and contracts, he helped build major Atlanta landmarks including Hartsfield-Jackson Airport and the Georgia Dome. He was also a dedicated civic leader focused on education and mentorship. Russell is interred at South-View Cemetery, a historic burial ground founded in 1886 by formerly enslaved African Americans.

Bazoline E. Usher was a trailblazing educator whose 40-year career in Atlanta Public Schools broke barriers for African American students and faculty. As the first African American to hold an office in Atlanta City Hall, she expanded opportunity and shaped the future of Black education in the segregated South.
Born into slavery, Herndon became Atlanta's first Black millionaire and founded Atlanta Life Insurance Company, one of the most successful Black-owned businesses in American history.

William H. Crogman was a distinguished educator and civic leader who served as a cornerstone of Atlanta's intellectual landscape, championing academic excellence and character development for the Black community. His visionary institutional leadership during a transformative era in the South fostered both educational progress and lasting civic growth.

Robert H. Grant was a skilled blacksmith and drayman who founded Zion Hill Baptist Church, embodying the vital intersection of labor and spiritual leadership in post-Civil War Atlanta. His life serves as a testament to the foundational role of the Black church in fostering community resilience and organizing African American life during Reconstruction.
